content.text: A speaker embedding is a fixed-dimensional vector that encodes the

of a speaker — independently of what they are saying. Given a few seconds of any utterance by person A, the speaker encoder produces the same vector (or a very similar one) regardless of the words spoken. Given audio from a different person B, it produces a distinctly different vector.
These vectors live in a metric space where

measures voice similarity. Same-speaker embeddings cluster tightly; different speakers cluster far apart. This property makes speaker embeddings the building block of speaker verification, diarisation, and — most relevant here —
: extract an embedding from a reference clip and use it to condition a TTS model to speak in that voice.

attrs.alt: Speaker embedding pipeline: reference audio through log-mel extraction, frame encoder, temporal pooling, L2 norm to 256-d embedding. Embedding space cluster plot, GE2E training loss diagram, applications panel, and model comparison table.

content.text: The pipeline has five stages: (1) extract 80-dim log-mel features per frame; (2) run a frame-level encoder (LSTM, TDNN, or Transformer) to produce per-frame hidden states; (3) aggregate across time via temporal pooling; (4) project and L2-normalise to the embedding dimension; (5) the resulting unit vector is the speaker embedding.
in 2014 as a deep learning replacement for i-vectors. The model is a 3-layer LSTM that processes a sequence of log-mel frames and pools their outputs. The 2018 follow-up introduced the
(Wan et al.), which jointly trains the embedding with an objective that directly optimises inter-speaker distance.
A training batch contains N speakers, each with M utterances. For speaker n, the centroid
is the mean embedding over its M utterances. The similarity matrix
is the i-th utterance embedding for speaker j, and
are learned scalars. The softmax loss pushes

This is equivalent to a cross-entropy loss where the correct class is "utterance belongs to its own speaker's centroid". Training with large N (typically 64) and M (typically 10) forces the encoder to learn compact, well-separated clusters for all speakers simultaneously.

(Snyder et al., 2018) uses a Time-Delay Neural Network (TDNN) encoder with statistical pooling. TDNN layers capture temporal context efficiently by applying 1-D convolutions with dilated receptive fields — each hidden unit sees frames at fixed offsets (e.g., t-2, t-1, t, t+1, t+2). The stats pooling layer computes the mean and standard deviation of frame-level outputs over the whole utterance, concatenating them to form a fixed-size representation.

ECAPA-TDNN (Emphasized Channel Attention, Propagation and Aggregation) extends x-vectors with three innovations: (1) Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) channel attention to re-weight feature maps, (2) multi-scale feature aggregation across layers, and (3) attentive statistics pooling (learned frame-level attention weights instead of uniform mean). ECAPA-TDNN achieves ~0.87% Equal Error Rate on VoxCeleb1 — a 5x improvement over x-vectors — and is the standard backbone in SpeechBrain.

# pip install speechbrain
import torchaudio
from speechbrain.inference.speaker import EncoderClassifier

# Pretrained ECAPA-TDNN trained on VoxCeleb2
classifier = EncoderClassifier.from_hparams(
    source="speechbrain/spkrec-ecapa-voxceleb",
    savedir="/tmp/ecapa-model",
)

# Load a WAV and extract embedding
waveform, sr = torchaudio.load("reference.wav")
# Model expects 16 kHz
if sr != 16000:
    waveform = torchaudio.functional.resample(waveform, sr, 16000)

# Returns tensor of shape (1, 192)
embedding = classifier.encode_batch(waveform)
embedding = embedding.squeeze(0)       # (192,)
print(embedding.shape)                 # torch.Size([192])

# Compare two speakers
wav2, _ = torchaudio.load("other.wav")
embedding2 = classifier.encode_batch(wav2).squeeze(0)

import torch
cosine_sim = torch.nn.functional.cosine_similarity(
    embedding.unsqueeze(0), embedding2.unsqueeze(0)
).item()
print(f"Cosine similarity: {cosine_sim:.4f}")
# Same speaker → ~0.85+, different → <0.5

Resemblyzer is a lightweight Python library wrapping the GE2E-trained LSTM speaker encoder from Google's Generalized End-to-End paper. It outputs 256-dim L2-normalised d-vectors and is the encoder used inside Coqui TTS and several voice cloning systems.

# pip install resemblyzer
import numpy as np
from resemblyzer import VoiceEncoder, preprocess_wav
from pathlib import Path

encoder = VoiceEncoder(device="cpu")  # or "cuda"

# Preprocess: resample to 16 kHz, trim silence, normalise
wav = preprocess_wav(Path("reference.wav"))

# Single utterance → 256-dim embedding
embed = encoder.embed_utterance(wav)    # np.ndarray shape (256,)
print(embed.shape, np.linalg.norm(embed))  # (256,) 1.0  (L2 norm = 1)

# Multiple clips → mean-pooled embedding (more robust)
clips = [preprocess_wav(Path(f"ref_{i}.wav")) for i in range(3)]
embeds = np.array([encoder.embed_utterance(c) for c in clips])
mean_embed = embeds.mean(axis=0)
mean_embed /= np.linalg.norm(mean_embed)   # re-normalise
print("Mean embed shape:", mean_embed.shape)  # (256,)

Using multiple reference clips and mean-pooling the embeddings produces a more stable representation than a single clip, especially for short references (<3 s). The embedding is then passed to a TTS model (e.g., XTTS v2) as the speaker conditioning vector.
Temporal Pooling: How Frames Become a Fixed Vector

A reference clip of 5 seconds at 10 ms frame-shift produces 500 frames. The frame encoder outputs a 512-dim hidden vector per frame. Temporal pooling collapses this variable-length sequence into a single fixed-dim vector. Three approaches:
Average of all frame vectors. Simple, works well for short clips. Sensitive to silence frames.
Concatenate mean and standard deviation. The std captures prosodic variability and improves discriminability, used in x-vector / ECAPA-TDNN.
A small MLP predicts a weight per frame; weighted mean is computed. Learns to focus on phonetically-rich frames and ignore silence, outperforming uniform mean for short references.
Speaker Verification and Equal Error Rate
Speaker verification is the binary task of deciding whether two audio clips come from the same speaker. The decision uses cosine similarity against a threshold
. As the threshold varies, we trace an ROC curve. The
is the threshold where the false-accept rate (impostors accepted) equals the false-reject rate (genuine speakers rejected). Lower EER is better.

# Compute EER for a set of trial pairs
import numpy as np
from scipy.interpolate import interp1d

def compute_eer(scores, labels):
    """
    scores: np.array of cosine similarities
    labels: np.array of 1 (same speaker) or 0 (different)
    Returns: (eer, threshold)
    """
    thresholds = np.linspace(scores.min(), scores.max(), 1000)
    frrs, fars = [], []
    for t in thresholds:
        predictions = (scores >= t).astype(int)
        tp = ((predictions == 1) & (labels == 1)).sum()
        fp = ((predictions == 1) & (labels == 0)).sum()
        fn = ((predictions == 0) & (labels == 1)).sum()
        tn = ((predictions == 0) & (labels == 0)).sum()
        frr = fn / (fn + tp + 1e-8)   # false reject rate
        far = fp / (fp + tn + 1e-8)   # false accept rate
        frrs.append(frr)
        fars.append(far)
    frrs = np.array(frrs)
    fars = np.array(fars)
    # EER: where |FRR - FAR| is minimum
    idx = np.argmin(np.abs(frrs - fars))
    eer = (frrs[idx] + fars[idx]) / 2
    return float(eer), float(thresholds[idx])

# Example usage
np.random.seed(0)
scores_same = np.random.normal(0.85, 0.05, 500)    # genuine pairs
scores_diff = np.random.normal(0.30, 0.10, 500)    # impostor pairs
all_scores = np.concatenate([scores_same, scores_diff])
labels = np.array([1]*500 + [0]*500)
eer, thr = compute_eer(all_scores, labels)
print(f"EER: {eer*100:.2f}%  threshold: {thr:.3f}")
In a speaker-conditioned TTS model, the speaker embedding
is injected into the decoder at every step. Common conditioning strategies:
to every decoder input step. Simple and effective for encoder-decoder TTS (Tacotron 2 speaker adaptation).
; modulate normalised activations. Used in NaturalSpeech 2 and VALL-E.
as a single-token key/value sequence; each decoder layer attends to it alongside the phoneme sequence. Used in XTTS v2.

# Zero-shot voice cloning with XTTS v2 using a speaker embedding
# pip install TTS
from TTS.api import TTS
import torch

tts = TTS("tts_models/multilingual/multi-dataset/xtts_v2").to(
    "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
)

# The model internally:
# 1. Extracts a GE2E speaker embedding from speaker_wav
# 2. Conditions the GPT-based decoder on the embedding
# 3. Generates codec tokens then reconstructs via HiFi-GAN
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="The voice encoder captures identity without any fine-tuning.",
    speaker_wav="reference.wav",    # 3–10 s clean reference clip
    language="en",
    file_path="cloned_output.wav",
)

Self-supervised models like WavLM and HuBERT, pre-trained on hundreds of thousands of hours, produce rich frame representations that encode both phonetic and speaker-related information. Fine-tuning a WavLM-Large model with AAM-Softmax on VoxCeleb achieves ~0.39% EER — the current state of the art.

Unlike ECAPA-TDNN, which is trained end-to-end for speaker discrimination, WavLM embeddings are general-purpose. The speaker-relevant information concentrates in the last few Transformer layers, so fine-tuning only those layers (while freezing the rest) is computationally efficient and avoids catastrophic forgetting.

# WavLM-based speaker embedding via s3prl
# pip install s3prl torch torchaudio
import torch, torchaudio
from s3prl.nn import S3PRLUpstream

upstream = S3PRLUpstream("wavlm_large").eval()  # downloads ~1.2 GB

wav, sr = torchaudio.load("reference.wav")
if sr != 16000:
    wav = torchaudio.functional.resample(wav, sr, 16000)

# Returns list of (B, T, D) hidden states, one per layer
with torch.no_grad():
    all_hs, all_lens = upstream([wav.squeeze(0)])

# Use last layer for speaker identity (layer 24 for WavLM-Large)
last_layer = all_hs[-1]   # (1, T, 1024)

# Mean-pool over time
spk_embed = last_layer.mean(dim=1)        # (1, 1024)
spk_embed = torch.nn.functional.normalize(spk_embed, dim=-1)
print(spk_embed.shape)    # torch.Size([1, 1024])

Speaker diarisation — 'who spoke when' — uses speaker embeddings as its core primitive. The pipeline is: (1) VAD to get speech segments, (2) extract embeddings per segment, (3) cluster embeddings (agglomerative clustering with cosine distance or spectral clustering), (4) assign speaker labels.

# Diarisation with pyannote.audio
# pip install pyannote.audio
from pyannote.audio import Pipeline

# Requires HuggingFace token (gated model)
pipeline = Pipeline.from_pretrained(
    "pyannote/speaker-diarization-3.1",
    use_auth_token="YOUR_HF_TOKEN",
)

# Run on file
diarization = pipeline("meeting.wav")

for turn, _, speaker in diarization.itertracks(yield_label=True):
    print(f"[{turn.start:5.1f}s - {turn.end:5.1f}s] {speaker}")

# Example output:
# [  0.5s -  12.3s] SPEAKER_00
# [ 12.8s -  25.1s] SPEAKER_01
# [ 25.4s -  38.0s] SPEAKER_00

Pyannote 3.1 uses a WavLM-based speaker embedding model with an EEND-based overlap detector. It achieves DER (Diarisation Error Rate) of ~12% on AMI meeting corpus without oracle VAD — significantly better than older ECAPA-TDNN pipelines.
Modern speaker encoders use Additive Angular Margin Softmax (AAM-Softmax, also called ArcFace) instead of standard cross-entropy. It modifies the decision boundary to enforce a minimum angular margin
between classes in the embedding space:
is the angle between the embedding and the correct class centroid,
is a scaling factor (typically 30). The margin forces intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, improving EER by ~20–30% over CE at no extra inference cost.
Speaker embeddings become more stable with longer references. Minimum 3 seconds; 10–30 seconds is ideal. For very short clips, average multiple embeddings from different time windows.
Phone microphone vs studio recording produces embeddings in different regions of the space, artificially inflating cosine distance. Channel compensation (PLDA or normalisation) helps.
Background music, reverberation, and overlapping speakers corrupt embeddings. Apply VAD before encoding and denoise if necessary.
Larger embedding dimensions (512, 1024) capture more speaker detail but require more data to generalise. 192–256-dim ECAPA embeddings are a practical sweet spot for most applications.
Models trained on VoxCeleb (celebrity YouTube) may degrade on read speech, children, or non-English accents. Fine-tuning on target-domain data (even a few hours) typically recovers performance.
Speaker embeddings are L2-normalised fixed-dim vectors encoding voice identity; cosine similarity in that space measures speaker similarity.
GE2E loss trains a speaker encoder by maximising intra-speaker cosine similarity (via centroids) and minimising inter-speaker similarity within each batch.
ECAPA-TDNN (speechbrain) and WavLM fine-tunes (wespeaker) are the current state-of-the-art open-source speaker encoders, achieving <1% EER on VoxCeleb1.
Resemblyzer provides a quick d-vector encoder for voice cloning demos; XTTS v2 accepts its 256-dim output directly as a speaker conditioning vector.
EER (Equal Error Rate) is the standard benchmark: the threshold where false-accept rate equals false-reject rate.
AAM-Softmax improves cluster separation by enforcing an angular margin between classes, yielding 20–30% relative EER gains over cross-entropy at zero inference-time cost.
